stem: You are the perinatal psychiatry registrar reviewing a 29-year-old woman with bipolar I disorder, stable for 4 years on sodium valproate 1000 mg daily as monotherapy with two prior depressive episodes and one manic episode in her early 20s. She has been in a stable relationship for 3 years and is now planning a first pregnancy within the next 6 to 12 months. She uses no other medications, has no contraindications to lithium, has had no thyroid or renal disease, and her family history is unremarkable. She has read patient information that suggests valproate carries risks in pregnancy and asks for your advice on what to do. She is a non-smoker, drinks no alcohol, and her partner is supportive of her decisions.

data_table.Examination: Euthymic affect, normal speech, no thought disorder, no perceptual disturbance, no parkinsonism, mood elevated to baseline range
data_table.Investigations: Routine bloods including TFTs, eGFR, valproate level 460 micromol/L (RR 300-700) all within target range

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Continue valproate at the current dose throughout pregnancy and add high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily preconceptionally, and arrange detailed second-trimester anatomy ultrasound to detect any neural-tube or other structural anomalies that may arise
options.rationale: Folic acid does not fully mitigate valproate teratogenicity; valproate use in pregnancy carries unacceptable risk in women planning conception when alternative effective agents exist. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

options.text: Continue valproate at a reduced dose of 500 mg daily and add lamotrigine titrated to 200 mg daily, hoping that the combination will protect against relapse while halving the dose-dependent teratogenic risk profile during pregnancy planning today
options.rationale: Risk reduction with valproate is not proportionate to dose reduction at clinically effective doses, and any continued valproate in pregnancy carries unacceptable teratogenicity when alternatives exist. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

options.text: Plan a switch from valproate to lithium pre-conception with overlap and gradual cross-titration, baseline thyroid and renal function with ongoing therapeutic drug monitoring, and high-dose folic acid 5 mg daily preconceptionally
options.rationale: Valproate carries a 10% major malformation rate (especially neural tube defects) and a 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental impact; pre-conception switch to lithium (preferred maintenance in childbearing women with bipolar I) with cross-titration and high-dose folate is recommended Australian practice. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

options.text: Switch from valproate to topiramate 100 mg twice daily as a non-teratogenic mood stabiliser alternative and arrange detailed pre-conception genetic counselling before any further changes to her medication regimen at this stage in her care
options.rationale: Topiramate is itself teratogenic (oral clefts, low birth weight), is not licensed as a mood stabiliser in Australia, and is not first-line in bipolar maintenance. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

options.text: Cease valproate abruptly and rely on lifestyle measures and structured psychological therapy for relapse prevention until she has completed her pregnancy and lactation period before resuming maintenance pharmacotherapy at any later point
options.rationale: Discontinuation of mood stabiliser in bipolar I carries high relapse risk, particularly in pregnancy and the postpartum period; treatment is changed to a safer agent rather than stopped. (Source: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement)
source_refs: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement

explanation.summary: Sodium valproate is the most teratogenic of the mood stabilisers, with 10% major malformation rate (notably neural tube defects), and 30 to 40% adverse neurodevelopmental outcomes (lower IQ, autism spectrum disorders). Australian regulators have implemented pregnancy prevention programs requiring documented contraception in women of childbearing potential. For pre-conception planning in bipolar I, lithium is the preferred maintenance agent (with anomaly scan and serial monitoring), with lamotrigine the alternative for predominantly depressive presentations. Cross-titration to lithium with overlap, baseline renal and thyroid function, high-dose preconception folate, and counselling about Ebstein anomaly (lithium first-trimester risk is modest at around 1 in 1000) are key.
key_points: Valproate: 10% major malformation rate, 30 to 40% neurodevelopmental impact; pregnancy prevention program required
Lithium: preferred maintenance in bipolar I planning pregnancy; Ebstein anomaly risk around 1 in 1000
Lamotrigine: alternative for predominantly depressive bipolar; safer than valproate but less effective for mania prevention
High-dose folate 5 mg daily preconceptionally for women on any anti-seizure medication
explanation.pearls: Folic acid does not rescue valproate teratogenicity - the combination is misleadingly reassuring and the patient still carries a 10% major malformation risk; switching to lithium pre-conception is the right ask, not adding folate.

sources.label: RANZCP: Perinatal Mental Health Position Statement
TGA: Valproate use and pregnancy prevention

stem: You are the acute psychiatry registrar reviewing a 22-year-old man brought in by police after he was found running through traffic shouting at unseen voices and accusing strangers of conspiring against him. Collateral from his housemate confirms 12 months of daily high-potency cannabis use with a sharp escalation in the past 3 weeks coinciding with a relationship breakdown. He has no prior psychiatric history, no family history of psychotic illness, no other substance use beyond occasional alcohol, no head injury, and a normal medical and neurological examination. Urine drug screen is positive for THC only. Acute behavioural disturbance has now settled with calm verbal de-escalation in the ED; he is paranoid but able to engage with the interview process today.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/84, HR 96, T 36.9 degrees C, SpO2 98% in air
data_table.Examination: Alert, paranoid, mild psychomotor agitation, conjunctival injection, no nystagmus or rigidity, no neurological deficit
data_table.Investigations: Urine drug screen: THC positive, all other drugs of abuse negative; routine bloods unremarkable; CT brain normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Discharge home with his housemate, prescribe diazepam 5 mg three times daily for 7 days for sedation and arrange follow-up with the local alcohol and other drug service within the next 2 weeks for cannabis cessation support
options.rationale: Active psychotic symptoms with paranoia and recent dangerous behaviour preclude direct discharge to a housemate without specialist follow-up; benzodiazepine monotherapy does not treat psychotic symptoms. (Source: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement)
source_refs: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement

options.text: Admit him under the Mental Health Act involuntarily for 4 weeks of inpatient stabilisation with risperidone 4 mg daily and a low-dose sodium valproate mood stabiliser added from admission as a substance-induced episode mood stabiliser
options.rationale: Involuntary admission is reserved for patients lacking capacity or with risk profiles unsafe for voluntary care; he is engaging with assessment, and mood stabiliser addition is not indicated in substance-induced psychosis. (Source: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement)
source_refs: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement

options.text: Discharge with a 4-week supply of olanzapine 10 mg daily and rely on his general practitioner to arrange specialist mental health follow-up, with reattendance only if there are any further psychotic symptoms after the discharge today
options.rationale: Direct discharge without specialist follow-up risks decompensation and undertreatment; assertive early intervention in psychosis with specialist follow-up improves long-term outcomes. (Source: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement)
source_refs: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement

options.text: Commence clozapine 12.5 mg daily titrated as inpatient and continue for at least 6 months given the severity of the psychotic episode and the high risk of relapse with ongoing cannabis use after his discharge from hospital
options.rationale: Clozapine is reserved for treatment-resistant schizophrenia after failure of two adequate trials of other antipsychotics; first-episode cannabis-induced psychosis should not be treated with clozapine. (Source: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement)
source_refs: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement

options.text: Admit him voluntarily for short-term observation, commence olanzapine 10 mg orally daily, arrange brief intervention and motivational interviewing for cannabis cessation, and plan early community mental health team follow-up after discharge
options.rationale: Acute cannabis-induced psychosis with significant symptoms warrants short admission, an antipsychotic to treat acute symptoms (olanzapine is a reasonable choice), cessation counselling, and structured follow-up given the elevated risk of progression to a primary psychotic illness. (Source: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement)
source_refs: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement

explanation.summary: Cannabis-induced psychotic disorder presents with paranoia, perceptual disturbances and disorganised behaviour in the context of cannabis use, classically with high-potency THC products and adolescent or young-adult onset. Acute management combines short admission for safety and observation, antipsychotic treatment (commonly olanzapine or risperidone), substance use intervention with motivational interviewing, and assertive early intervention follow-up. A substantial proportion (around 20 to 40%) of cannabis-induced psychoses progress to a schizophrenia-spectrum disorder over the following decade, particularly with ongoing cannabis use, so longitudinal follow-up is essential.
key_points: Cannabis-induced psychosis: paranoia and perceptual disturbances with cannabis use, especially high-potency THC
Acute management: short voluntary admission, antipsychotic (olanzapine or risperidone), cessation intervention
Risk of progression to primary psychotic illness: 20 to 40% over the next decade
Assertive early intervention follow-up improves long-term function and reduces relapse
explanation.pearls: Even when psychosis is clearly substance-induced, the appropriate disposition is short admission with antipsychotic and early intervention follow-up - not direct discharge - because the cumulative risk of progression to schizophrenia warrants longitudinal engagement.

sources.label: RANZCP: Substance use and psychosis position statement

subtopic_detail: Gamma-hydroxybutyrate withdrawal recognition and inpatient management
stem: You are the addiction medicine registrar called to ED for a 32-year-old man with a 4-year history of daily gamma-hydroxybutyrate use, dosing every 2 to 3 hours including overnight, who ran out of supply 18 hours ago. He has been profoundly anxious, tremulous and diaphoretic; in the last 4 hours he has become confused with visual hallucinations of insects on the wall and a brief generalised seizure self-terminating after 60 seconds. Standard alcohol-withdrawal-style diazepam loading at 60 mg over the past 2 hours has produced only modest improvement in his agitation and autonomic features. He has no past psychiatric history, no other substance use beyond moderate alcohol, and no metabolic, neurological or infective focus identified on a comprehensive bedside workup.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 168/104, HR 132, T 38.3 degrees C, SpO2 96% in air, RR 24
data_table.Examination: Confused, GCS 14 with intermittent worsening, diaphoretic, coarse tremor, hyperreflexia without sustained clonus, visual hallucinations
data_table.Investigations: Routine bloods, CK, lactate, BSL, electrolytes within acceptable limits; CT brain normal; LP not yet performed; urine drug screen GHB-positive

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer haloperidol 5 mg intramuscularly with promethazine for behavioural control and arrange a CT brain and lumbar puncture before any further sedation is given, in case there is an undetected intracranial cause for delirium
options.rationale: Antipsychotics lower seizure threshold and worsen delirium in GHB withdrawal; the syndrome is recognised clinically and sedation with high-dose benzodiazepines plus baclofen is the appropriate next step. (Source: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management)
source_refs: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management

options.text: Escalate to high-dose benzodiazepine loading guided by sedation and seizure response, add a GABAergic adjunct such as baclofen, and discuss urgent ICU transfer for ongoing sedation, airway monitoring and ventilation if required
options.rationale: Severe GHB withdrawal is benzodiazepine-resistant and progresses rapidly to seizures, delirium and autonomic instability; high-dose benzodiazepines with GABAergic adjuncts (baclofen) and ICU admission for airway management are the recognised escalation pathway. (Source: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management)
source_refs: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management

options.text: Commence clonidine 75 microgram four times daily and dexmedetomidine infusion in a monitored bed, with hourly observations and a low threshold for repeating his routine bloods overnight if his agitation has not improved on this regimen
options.rationale: Alpha-2 agonists alone do not control GHB withdrawal-related seizures and delirium; the central problem is GABA-B underactivity and benzodiazepine-resistant agitation, requiring high-dose benzodiazepines and GABAergic adjuncts. (Source: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management)
source_refs: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management

options.text: Commence intravenous magnesium sulfate 4 g over 30 minutes and intravenous thiamine 300 mg daily for 5 days as the primary pharmacological intervention for his withdrawal syndrome and assess his progress 12 hours after this loading regimen
options.rationale: Magnesium and thiamine are supportive only; severe GHB withdrawal requires high-dose benzodiazepines, GABAergic adjuncts and ICU-level monitoring rather than magnesium and thiamine as the primary intervention. (Source: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management)
source_refs: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management

options.text: Discharge him to a community withdrawal facility with twice-daily nursing review and oral diazepam taper over the next 7 days, with explicit instructions about return precautions for seizures or worsening behaviour after the discharge
options.rationale: Severe GHB withdrawal with seizures and delirium has a mortality risk and is not safe for community withdrawal; it requires inpatient and often intensive care management with airway support available. (Source: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management)
source_refs: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management

explanation.summary: GHB withdrawal in heavy daily users can begin within 4 to 6 hours of last dose, escalates rapidly over 24 to 72 hours, and produces an alcohol-withdrawal-like syndrome that is characteristically benzodiazepine-resistant with delirium, autonomic instability and seizures. Management is inpatient with high-dose benzodiazepine loading guided by sedation and seizure response, GABAergic adjuncts (baclofen or, in some centres, pharmaceutical sodium oxybate where available), and ICU-level monitoring for airway and ventilation. Antipsychotics worsen the delirium and lower seizure threshold. Mortality without appropriate inpatient management is significant.
key_points: GHB withdrawal in heavy daily users: onset 4 to 6 hours, peak 24 to 72 hours, prolonged course
Classically benzodiazepine-resistant; high-dose loading with baclofen adjunct often required
ICU monitoring for airway, autonomic instability and prolonged sedation requirements
Avoid antipsychotics: lower seizure threshold, worsen delirium; magnesium and thiamine are supportive only
explanation.pearls: If 60 mg of diazepam over 2 hours has barely touched the agitation in a daily GHB user, do not just keep stepping benzodiazepines - add baclofen and call ICU early, because the syndrome can progress to refractory delirium and respiratory arrest within hours.

sources.label: Australian Clinical Guidelines: GHB withdrawal management

subtopic_detail: Anticoagulation duration after unprovoked pulmonary embolism
stem: You are the general medicine registrar reviewing a 58-year-old man at his 3-month follow-up after an unprovoked submassive pulmonary embolism initially treated with rivaroxaban 15 mg twice daily for 21 days followed by 20 mg daily ongoing. He has had no recurrent venous thromboembolism, no significant bleeding episodes, normal renal function, no active malignancy, no antiphospholipid antibodies, and a normal first-degree family history. His current symptoms have resolved and he has returned to full-time work. His PESA-style bleeding risk assessment is low. He understands the recurrence risk discussion and asks about whether to stop his anticoagulation now that 3 months have passed without any complications during the initial treatment course.

data_table.Examination: Cardiovascular and respiratory examination unremarkable, no calf swelling or tenderness, oxygen saturations 98% in air at rest
data_table.Investigations: eGFR 88, Hb 142, platelets 220 x10^9/L; thrombophilia screen negative; CT chest at 3 months shows partial clot resolution

lead_in: What is the most appropriate ongoing anticoagulation plan?

options.text: Switch from rivaroxaban to warfarin with a target international normalised ratio of 2.0 to 3.0 for indefinite anticoagulation and arrange weekly INR monitoring until the patient is stable on the new anticoagulant regimen
options.rationale: DOACs are first-line and at least as effective as warfarin for VTE secondary prevention without monitoring; switching to warfarin in a stable patient introduces inconvenience and bleeding risk without benefit. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance

options.text: Continue rivaroxaban at the reduced extended dose of 10 mg once daily indefinitely with annual review of bleeding risk, renal function and the ongoing balance of recurrence risk against bleeding risk to inform continuation
options.rationale: Unprovoked PE has a high recurrence risk (around 10% in year 1, 30 to 40% at 5 years off therapy); current Australian guidance favours indefinite anticoagulation in low-bleed-risk patients, with reduced-dose rivaroxaban (10 mg daily) or apixaban (2.5 mg twice daily) for extended therapy. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance

options.text: Cease all anticoagulation at 3 months and provide a written plan to recommence rivaroxaban if there are any new symptoms suggesting recurrence, with annual D-dimer testing as a surveillance strategy for asymptomatic recurrence
options.rationale: Ceasing anticoagulation at 3 months after an unprovoked PE leaves the patient with an unacceptably high recurrence rate; routine surveillance D-dimer does not replace ongoing pharmacological prophylaxis in this context. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance

options.text: Stop rivaroxaban now and prescribe aspirin 100 mg daily as secondary prevention for the next 12 months, with a planned VTE recurrence review at 12 months to determine whether further intervention is required at that time
options.rationale: Aspirin provides only modest VTE recurrence reduction and is markedly inferior to extended-dose DOAC; it is not a satisfactory substitute when extended anticoagulation is otherwise appropriate. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance

options.text: Continue rivaroxaban 20 mg once daily indefinitely without dose reduction and arrange follow-up CT pulmonary angiography in 12 months to assess for chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension and adjust treatment accordingly
options.rationale: Extended-dose reduction (rivaroxaban 10 mg or apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily) is non-inferior for recurrence and lowers bleeding risk; routine surveillance CTPA at 12 months is not standard in an asymptomatic patient with normal saturations and exercise tolerance. (Source: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance)
source_refs: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance

explanation.summary: After 3 months of treatment-dose anticoagulation for an unprovoked PE, decision-making centres on the recurrence-versus-bleeding balance. Unprovoked PE carries around 10% recurrence in year 1 and 30 to 40% at 5 years off therapy. For low-bleed-risk patients, indefinite anticoagulation is preferred; the AMPLIFY-EXT and EINSTEIN-CHOICE trials support reduced-dose extended therapy (rivaroxaban 10 mg daily or apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily) as non-inferior for recurrence with a halved bleeding rate. Aspirin is markedly inferior. Annual reassessment of bleeding risk and renal function guides ongoing therapy.
key_points: Unprovoked PE recurrence: ~10% year 1, ~30 to 40% at 5 years off anticoagulation
Extended therapy: rivaroxaban 10 mg daily or apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily, non-inferior to full-dose with lower bleeding
Provoked PE with reversible risk factor: 3 months total anticoagulation usually sufficient
Annual review: bleeding risk, renal function, ongoing recurrence-risk vs bleeding balance
explanation.pearls: After an unprovoked PE in a low-bleed-risk patient, the default plan is indefinite anticoagulation at extended-reduced dose - aspirin is a fallback only when the patient declines DOAC, not an equivalent option.

sources.label: Thrombosis and Haemostasis Society of Australia and New Zealand: VTE guidance

subtopic_detail: Early rheumatoid arthritis DMARD initiation with methotrexate
stem: You are the rheumatology registrar in clinic reviewing a 46-year-old woman with 10 weeks of progressive symmetrical polyarthritis of the metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints and wrists, with 90 minutes of early morning stiffness. She has no prior medical history, takes no regular medications, and is up to date with cervical screening. Her bloods show a markedly elevated rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibody, raised inflammatory markers, normal renal and liver function, and a normal full blood count. Plain hand films are without bony erosion at this point. She is keen to start treatment, lives within 30 minutes of the clinic, has reliable transport, has no plans for pregnancy in the next 12 months and is willing to use reliable contraception during treatment.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/76, HR 76, T 37.0 degrees C, BMI 25
data_table.Examination: Bilateral symmetrical MCP and PIP synovitis, both wrists synovitic, no nodules, no extra-articular features, no skin rash
data_table.Investigations: RF 320 IU/mL (RR under 15), anti-CCP 198 U/mL (RR under 20), ESR 64, CRP 38; normal Hb, WCC, platelets, eGFR, LFTs; HBV/HCV/HIV serology negative, latent TB screen negative, varicella immune

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial disease-modifying anti-rheumatic drug regimen?

options.text: Commence long-term prednisolone 15 mg daily as monotherapy with calcium and vitamin D and a bisphosphonate for bone protection, with plans for slow gradual taper over the next 12 to 24 months as her symptoms improve over time
options.rationale: Long-term steroid monotherapy in RA causes significant morbidity (diabetes, osteoporosis, infections) without preventing joint damage; steroid is used only as a short bridge while DMARD takes effect. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines)
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines

options.text: Defer DMARD therapy and commence celecoxib 200 mg twice daily with regular paracetamol 1 g four times daily, plan a 6-month review with a repeat clinical assessment before any decision about disease-modifying therapy is made
options.rationale: Early DMARD initiation within a window of opportunity (under 3 months from symptom onset) improves long-term outcomes; NSAID monotherapy fails to prevent joint damage in seropositive disease. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines)
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines

options.text: Commence subcutaneous adalimumab 40 mg fortnightly as first-line biologic monotherapy given her highly seropositive disease and the elevated inflammatory markers to maximise rapid suppression of the inflammatory disease burden
options.rationale: Biologic DMARDs are reserved for inadequate response to conventional DMARD therapy after at least 6 months (typically methotrexate plus another csDMARD) under PBS criteria; not first-line. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines)
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines

options.text: Commence triple oral therapy with hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily, sulfasalazine 1 g twice daily and leflunomide 20 mg daily from the outset to maximise control of disease activity in this newly diagnosed early polyarthritis case
options.rationale: Triple therapy from outset is not first-line in Australia; methotrexate monotherapy with bridging steroid and rapid uptitration with intensified treatment if remission is not achieved at 3 months is preferred. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines)
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines

options.text: Commence oral methotrexate starting at 10 mg weekly with uptitration to 20 to 25 mg weekly over 8 weeks, prescribe folic acid 5 mg weekly on a non-methotrexate day, and bridge with a short prednisolone taper for symptom control
options.rationale: Methotrexate is first-line DMARD for early seropositive rheumatoid arthritis; rapid uptitration with weekly folate and short-term oral steroid bridging is standard practice with frequent disease activity reassessment. (Source: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines)
source_refs: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines

explanation.summary: Early seropositive rheumatoid arthritis should be treated with a conventional synthetic DMARD within a window of opportunity, ideally within 3 months of symptom onset, to prevent erosive joint damage. Methotrexate (oral or subcutaneous) is first-line, started at 10 to 15 mg weekly and uptitrated to 20 to 25 mg weekly over 4 to 8 weeks, with weekly folic acid 5 mg on a non-methotrexate day. Short prednisolone bridging (15 to 20 mg with taper over 8 to 12 weeks) controls symptoms while methotrexate takes effect. Pre-treatment workup includes HBV, HCV, HIV and latent TB serology. Biologics are reserved for inadequate response under PBS criteria.
key_points: Methotrexate first-line; start 10 to 15 mg weekly, uptitrate to 20 to 25 mg over 4 to 8 weeks
Weekly folic acid 5 mg on a non-methotrexate day reduces mucositis, hepatotoxicity and gastrointestinal side effects
Short prednisolone bridge while methotrexate takes effect (8 to 12 weeks then taper off)
Pre-treatment screen: HBV, HCV, HIV, latent TB, varicella immunity, baseline LFTs and FBC
explanation.pearls: If oral methotrexate is not achieving remission at maximum tolerated dose, switch to subcutaneous methotrexate before adding biologics - subcutaneous has higher bioavailability and rescues a meaningful proportion of inadequate oral responders.

sources.label: Australian Rheumatology Association: RA management guidelines
